title: Conditions (If / Else / Switch)
description: Master decision-making in Java -- if, else if, else, switch, nested conditions, and guard clauses -- with 60 hands-on exercises designed for neurodivergent learners.
learning_outcomes: Write if statements to make single decisions based on boolean conditions
Chain if / else if / else to handle multiple exclusive outcomes
Nest conditions inside each other for complex multi-level decisions
Use switch statements for clean multi-value matching on int, char, and String
Apply enhanced switch expressions (Java 14+) with arrow syntax for concise code
Combine comparison and logical operators in complex condition expressions
Write guard clauses to handle edge cases early and keep code flat
Avoid common condition bugs: dangling else, missing braces, = vs ==
Choose between if/else and switch for readability and maintainability
Solve real-world problems like grading, validation, menus, and access control

ai_tutor_config.personality: Encouraging, patient, uses real-world analogies like traffic lights, restaurant menus, and doorways. Never judges. Celebrates small wins. Re-explains in different ways when needed.

intervention_triggers.idle_40_seconds: Hey! Conditions can feel like a maze at first. Want me to draw out the decision path? I can trace which branch your code takes step by step.
intervention_triggers.idle_90_seconds: No rush! Try this: read the condition out loud in English. 'If age is greater than or equal to 18, then...' Often makes it click. Want me to help translate?
intervention_triggers.idle_180_seconds: Still here for you! Sometimes stepping away for 30 seconds helps. When you come back, I will walk through the logic like a flowchart -- one decision at a time.
intervention_triggers.first_error: Not quite right, but totally normal! Conditions are about LOGIC, and logic takes practice. Let me show you which branch the code actually took and why.
intervention_triggers.repeated_errors: This one is tricky! Let me explain a completely different way. Forget the code -- imagine you are a security guard at a building checking people in...
intervention_triggers.after_success: Brilliant! You traced the logic perfectly. Decision-making is the heart of programming, and you are getting really good at it!

encouragement_phrases: Great logic! You traced that perfectly!
You are thinking like a computer -- step by step!
Nice work! Conditions are the brain of every program.
Well done! Tricky decision tree and you nailed it.
Perfect! Your if/else logic is spot on.
Excellent! Right structure for this problem.
Awesome! You avoided the common traps.
That is exactly how a professional writes it!
Fantastic! Ready for a more complex decision?

explanation_styles.analogy: Think of conditions like a road with forks. At each fork, you check a sign (condition). TRUE: go left. FALSE: go right. Nested ifs are forks within forks.
explanation_styles.visual: I draw the decision tree: START -> check condition -> TRUE path or FALSE path. Each path can lead to another check or an action.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: I trace from top to bottom. First, what does the condition evaluate to? If true, we enter the block. If false, we skip to else. One decision at a time.
explanation_styles.code_trace: I become Java. I evaluate the condition, show TRUE or FALSE, then show exactly which block runs and which gets skipped.
explanation_styles.comparison: Side by side: if/else vs switch vs ternary for the same problem. See which is clearest for each situation.

lessons.title: Java Conditions -- Making Decisions in Code

theory.introduction:
Every program needs to make decisions. Should the user see this page? Is the password correct? Has the player run out of lives? These yes/no questions are answered by CONDITIONS -- the brain of your programs.

Real-world analogy: Imagine you are driving. You constantly make decisions: Is the light red? STOP. Is there a pedestrian? WAIT. Is the road clear? GO. Your brain evaluates conditions and takes different actions based on the answers. Java conditions work exactly the same way.

In this lesson, you will master every decision-making tool Java offers: the simple if statement, the two-path if/else, the multi-branch else-if chain, the value-matching switch statement, and the modern enhanced switch expression. You will also learn professional patterns like guard clauses and Boolean Zen that keep your code clean and bug-free.

By the end, you will be able to write programs that make intelligent decisions, validate inputs safely, handle edge cases gracefully, and avoid the sneaky bugs that trip up beginners.
theory.why_important:
Conditions are the most fundamental building block of useful software. Without them, programs can only do one thing -- follow a straight line from top to bottom. With conditions, programs become INTELLIGENT: they adapt, respond, validate, and decide.

Every login form checks your password (condition). Every game checks if you are alive (condition). Every shop checks if you can afford an item (condition). Every form validates your input (conditions). Every website checks your permissions (conditions).

Mastering conditions means you can: build interactive programs that respond to user input, write secure validation that rejects bad data, implement complex business rules clearly, and debug logic errors that cause wrong behaviour. This is where programming goes from 'printing text' to 'solving real problems'.

key_concepts.concept: The if Statement -- Your First Decision
key_concepts.explanation:
The if statement is the most fundamental decision in programming. It says: 'IF this condition is true, THEN execute this block of code.' If the condition is false, the block is simply skipped.

Real-world analogy: Think of an umbrella decision. IF it is raining, THEN take an umbrella. If it is not raining, you just walk past the umbrella stand -- nothing happens. The key insight is that the code inside the curly braces {} only runs when the condition in the parentheses () is true.

The condition must be a boolean expression -- something that evaluates to true or false. You can use comparison operators (age >= 18), logical operators (hasTicket && onTime), or even a boolean variable directly (if (isReady)).

CRITICAL: Always use curly braces {} even for single-line if blocks. Skipping braces is legal but causes subtle bugs when you add a second line later -- it will NOT be part of the if block even though the indentation suggests it is.

Practice tip: Read your conditions out loud in English. 'If score is greater than or equal to 50' makes it easy to spot logic errors.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Read it as English: 'If temperature is greater than 30, print the hot message.' The last line ALWAYS prints regardless.

key_concepts.explanation:
Sometimes you need to do one thing if a condition is true and a DIFFERENT thing if it is false. That is what else provides: a guaranteed alternative path.

Real-world analogy: A traffic light. IF the light is green, THEN drive. ELSE (it is not green), stop. There is no third option -- you always do one or the other. Every car makes exactly ONE choice.

The else block catches EVERYTHING that does not match the if condition. There is no condition on else -- it is the 'otherwise' or 'default' path.

IMPORTANT: Only ONE block executes. If the if-condition is true, the else block is completely skipped. If false, the if-block is skipped and else runs. Never both. Never neither (one always runs).

Common mistake: Writing two separate if statements instead of if/else. Two separate ifs are INDEPENDENT checks -- both could run! if/else is EXCLUSIVE -- exactly one runs.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Exactly ONE block runs. If true: first block. If false: else block. Never both, never neither.

key_concepts.concept: if / else if / else -- The Decision Chain
key_concepts.explanation:
When you have MORE than two options, chain them with else if. Java checks each condition top to bottom and runs the FIRST one that is true. All remaining conditions are skipped.

Real-world analogy: A restaurant menu with price tiers. IF you have over 50 pounds, eat at a restaurant. ELSE IF you have over 20 pounds, get takeaway. ELSE IF you have over 5 pounds, get a snack. ELSE you go hungry. The waiter checks your wallet once, top to bottom, and stops at the first option you can afford.

KEY INSIGHT: Order matters! Put the most specific or restrictive conditions FIRST. If you check score >= 50 before score >= 90, a score of 95 would match >= 50 first and never reach the >= 90 check.

The final else is optional but recommended -- it handles unexpected cases and prevents silent failures. Think of it as a safety net that catches anything you did not anticipate.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Order matters! Check highest first. Only the FIRST matching branch runs. 75 hits >=70 -> C.

key_concepts.explanation:
Sometimes one decision depends on another. You can put an if inside another if -- this is called nesting. The inner if only runs when the outer if is true.

Real-world analogy: Airport security. FIRST, do you have a boarding pass? IF yes, THEN check: do you have valid ID? IF yes, THEN check: any prohibited items? Each check only happens if the previous one passed.

Nesting is powerful but dangerous for readability. More than 2-3 levels deep makes code very hard to follow. When you find yourself nesting deeply, consider:
  1. Guard clauses (check and return/break early)
  2. Combining conditions with && instead of nesting
  3. Extracting logic into separate methods

TIP: if (A) { if (B) { ... } } is logically identical to if (A && B) { ... }. The && version is usually cleaner.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Nesting more than 2-3 levels deep? Flatten with && or guard clauses. Cleaner code = fewer bugs.

key_concepts.explanation:
When you are comparing ONE variable against MANY exact values, switch is cleaner than a long if/else if chain. It says: 'Take this value and MATCH it against these cases.'

Real-world analogy: A vending machine. You press button A, B, C, or D. The machine does not check ranges -- it matches your EXACT button press to a specific product. Each button (case) has a specific action.

Switch works with: int, char, String, byte, short, and enum types. It does NOT work with: double, float, long, boolean, or ranges.

CRITICAL: The break statement! Without break, Java 'falls through' to the NEXT case. This is intentional in the language design but catches many beginners off guard. Always include break unless you deliberately want fall-through.

The default case is like else -- it catches any value that does not match a specific case. Always include it as a safety net.
key_concepts.ai_hint: switch matches EXACT values (not ranges). Always use break! Forgetting it causes fall-through bugs.

key_concepts.explanation:
Since Java 7, switch can match Strings (case-sensitive). And sometimes fall-through is USEFUL -- when multiple cases should do the same thing.

Real-world analogy: A receptionist routing calls. 'Sales' and 'Marketing' both go to floor 3. 'Engineering' and 'IT' both go to floor 5. Instead of writing separate directions for each, you group them together.

Fall-through pattern for grouping: write multiple case labels without break between them. They all 'fall into' the same code block.

String matching is CASE-SENSITIVE: 'Monday' does NOT match 'monday'. Use .toLowerCase() or .toUpperCase() before the switch if you want case-insensitive matching.

Null Strings cause NullPointerException in switch. Always check for null before switching on a String.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Group cases for same action: case "a": case "b": (no break between). String match is CASE-SENSITIVE.

key_concepts.explanation:
Modern Java has a cleaner switch syntax using arrows (->). No break needed, no fall-through risk, and it can RETURN a value.

The arrow syntax: case VALUE -> action; replaces the old case VALUE: action; break; pattern.

You can also use switch as an EXPRESSION that produces a value, assigned to a variable:
  String result = switch(day) { case 1 -> "Mon"; case 2 -> "Tue"; default -> "Other"; };

For multi-line cases, use a block with yield:
  case 1 -> { String s = "Monday"; yield s; }

This is the PREFERRED style in modern Java. It is shorter, safer (no fall-through bugs), and more readable.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Arrow switch: no break needed, no fall-through, can return values. case 6, 7 -> groups values.

key_concepts.concept: Guard Clauses -- Fail Fast, Stay Flat
key_concepts.explanation:
Guard clauses check for invalid or edge-case conditions FIRST and return/exit early. This keeps the main logic at the top level with no deep nesting.

Real-world analogy: A bouncer at a club. Instead of one mega-check ('Are you old enough AND have ID AND not banned AND sober enough?'), the bouncer does quick sequential checks: 'No ID? Go home. Under 18? Go home. Banned? Go home. OK, you are in.' Each failed check exits immediately.

Pattern: Instead of wrapping the entire method in if (valid) { ... }, flip it: if (!valid) { return; } then write the main logic below with no nesting.

Benefits: Code reads top to bottom. No deep nesting. Easy to add new checks. Each validation is independent and clear. This is a PROFESSIONAL best practice used in every codebase.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Guard clause = check bad input FIRST, exit early. Main logic stays flat. Professional pattern!
Name your booleans! boolean isAdult = age >= 18; makes complex conditions readable.

key_concepts.concept: Common Condition Bugs -- What Goes Wrong
key_concepts.explanation:
TOP condition bugs that trip up beginners:

BUG 1: = vs == inside if. if (x = 5) ASSIGNS 5 to x. You wanted if (x == 5) to COMPARE.

BUG 2: Dangling else. Without braces, else attaches to the NEAREST if, not the one you intended.

BUG 3: Missing braces. if (x > 5) doA(); doB(); -- doB() ALWAYS runs! It looks indented but is NOT inside the if.

BUG 4: Forgetting break in switch. Without break, execution falls through to the next case.

BUG 5: String comparison with ==. Use .equals(), not == for Strings in conditions.

BUG 6: Unreachable else if. Checking score >= 50 before score >= 90 means the 90 branch never executes.

BUG 7: Empty if body. if (x > 5); { ... } -- the semicolon makes the if do NOTHING. The block always runs.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Always use braces {}! Check for: = vs ==, semicolons after if(), missing break in switch, .equals() for Strings.

key_concepts.concept: Ternary vs If/Else -- When to Use Which
key_concepts.explanation:
The ternary operator (condition ? a : b) is a one-line if/else that returns a VALUE. Use it for SIMPLE assignments. Use full if/else for anything complex or multi-line.

Guidelines:
  USE ternary when: assigning one of two values, printing one of two messages, simple inline decisions.
  USE if/else when: executing multiple statements, complex nested logic, readability matters more than brevity.

Nested ternary (a ? b : c ? d : e) works but gets hard to read fast. If you nest more than once, switch to if/else.

Professional rule: If someone reading your code needs more than 2 seconds to understand the ternary, use if/else instead.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Ternary for simple 2-choice values. If/else for complex logic. When in doubt, use if/else.

key_concepts.explanation:
Many beginners write unnecessarily complex conditions. Boolean Zen means writing the simplest possible condition.

BAD:  if (isReady == true) -> GOOD: if (isReady)
BAD:  if (isReady == false) -> GOOD: if (!isReady)
BAD:  if (x > 5) { return true; } else { return false; } -> GOOD: return x > 5;
BAD:  boolean result = (x > 5) ? true : false; -> GOOD: boolean result = x > 5;

The comparison already IS a boolean. Comparing a boolean to true/false is like asking 'Is true true?' -- redundant.

Another pattern: instead of if (list.size() > 0), many Java classes have isEmpty(): if (!list.isEmpty()). More readable and more intentional.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Never write == true or == false. The boolean IS the condition. Simplify!

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Simple If -- Temperature Check
complete_examples.description: Basic if statement with one condition.
complete_examples.output:
It is hot! Stay hydrated.
Temperature: 35C
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line by line: temp=35. First if: 35>30 is TRUE, so hot message prints. Second if: 35<10 is FALSE, so cold message is SKIPPED. Last line always prints. Note these are two SEPARATE if statements, not if/else -- both are checked independently.

Key takeaway: Basic if statements are independent checks. The condition must be boolean (true/false).
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Change temp to 5. Which messages print now? Then try temp=20 (neither hot nor cold).

complete_examples.title: Example 2: If/Else -- Voting Age
complete_examples.output:
Too young to vote.
Wait 2 more years.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
age=16. Is 16>=18? FALSE. So we skip the if-block entirely and jump to else. Inside else: 18-16=2, so wait 2 years. ONLY the else block ran. If age were 18+, only the if block would run. Never both.

Key takeaway: If/else guarantees exactly ONE of the two blocks runs. Perfect for binary decisions.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Change age to 18 (edge case). Then 17. Then 100. Verify the right block runs each time.

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Grade Calculator -- if/else if/else Chain
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
score=82. Check >=90? No (82<90). Check >=80? YES (82>=80). STOP HERE -- grade='B'. The >=70, >=60, else are ALL SKIPPED. Only the first matching branch runs.

Order matters: if we checked >=60 first, 82>=60 would be true and we would get D instead of B!

Key takeaway: In an else-if chain, conditions are checked top-down. First match wins. Check most restrictive first.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Test boundary values: 90, 80, 70, 60, 59. Each should give a different grade.

complete_examples.title: Example 4: Nested Conditions -- Cinema Entry
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Film is 18-rated, so we enter the outer if. Age 14: not >=18, so first inner fails. But withParent=true AND age>=12 (14>=12), so second inner matches: allowed with parent.

Key takeaway: Nested ifs create multi-level decision trees. Each level narrows the possibilities.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Change withParent to false. Then change age to 18. Then film to 'PG'. Trace each.

complete_examples.title: Example 5: switch Statement -- Day of Week
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
day=4. Switch checks: case 1? No. case 2? No. case 3? No. case 4? YES! name='Thursday', break exits switch. Cases 5-7 and default are skipped.

Key takeaway: switch matches ONE exact value. break exits. default catches unmatched values.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Remove the break after case 4. What happens? This demonstrates fall-through!

complete_examples.title: Example 6: switch with String -- Menu System
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
choice.toLowerCase() ensures case-insensitive matching. Multiple cases grouped without break between them: 'start', 'begin', 'go' all do the same thing. 'help' matches the help/? group.

Key takeaway: Group cases for same action. Use toLowerCase() for case-insensitive String matching.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add 'restart' as another alias for start. What about adding 'h' as alias for help?

complete_examples.title: Example 7: Enhanced switch (Java 14+)
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Arrow syntax (->): no break needed, no fall-through risk. Comma-separated values (6,7,8) match any of those. The switch is an EXPRESSION -- it returns a value assigned to season.

Key takeaway: Modern Java switch is cleaner, safer, and can return values directly.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a calculation: days in month using enhanced switch. February = 28 for now.

complete_examples.title: Example 8: Guard Clauses -- Flat Code
complete_examples.description: Early returns instead of deep nesting.
complete_examples.output:
Welcome, admin!
No username
Weak password
Account locked
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Each guard checks ONE bad condition and returns early. No nesting! The 'happy path' (successful login) is the last line, clean and flat. Compare this to nested ifs -- much easier to read and extend.

Key takeaway: Guard clauses = check bad input early, return immediately. Main logic stays flat.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add a guard for: username cannot contain spaces. Where does it go in the chain?

complete_examples.title: Example 9: Complex Conditions -- Loan Approval
complete_examples.output:
Age OK: true
Credit OK: true
Income OK: true
Employed: true
APPROVED: true
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Named booleans break complex logic into readable pieces. Each check is independent and testable. The final approved combines all four with &&.

Key takeaway: Extract conditions into named booleans for readability. Complex logic becomes self-documenting.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Change credit to 600 (below 650). What changes? Then income to 20000.

complete_examples.title: Example 10: switch Fall-Through Bug and Fix
complete_examples.output:
=== BUG (no break) ===
Silver
Gold
Unknown
=== FIXED ===
Silver
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Without break: case 2 matches, prints Silver, then FALLS THROUGH to case 3 (Gold) and default (Unknown). With break: case 2 matches, prints Silver, break exits. HUGE difference!

Key takeaway: Missing break causes fall-through. Always include break unless you intend to group cases.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: In the buggy version, what prints for level=1? It prints ALL four lines!

complete_examples.title: Example 11: Ternary vs If/Else Comparison
complete_examples.output:
Ternary: Adult
If/else: Adult
Full price
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Both give 'Adult' for age=20. Ternary is one line. If/else is four lines but clearer for beginners. For 3+ options (senior/full/child), if/else is the right choice -- ternary would be unreadable.

Key takeaway: Ternary for simple 2-way assignments. If/else for everything else.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Rewrite the 3-option pricing as nested ternary. Is it readable? Usually not!

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Boolean Zen in Action
complete_examples.output:
V1: active
V2: has items
Z1: active
Z2: has items
Even: true
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
== true is redundant. 'active' is already boolean! Same with 'count>0' -- it IS a boolean. The isEven method returns the comparison directly -- no if/else needed.

Key takeaway: Comparisons produce booleans. Do not compare booleans to booleans.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Find 3 places in your past code where you used == true. Simplify them!

complete_examples.title: Example 13: Multi-Condition Validation
complete_examples.output:
Email valid: true
Pass valid: true
Age valid: true
Registration successful!
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Each validation is a named boolean with short-circuit safety (null check first with &&). Final check combines all three. If ANY is false, registration fails.

Key takeaway: Validate inputs with named booleans. Check for null FIRST using && short-circuit.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Make email null. Does it crash? No -- the && short-circuits after null check!

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Real-World Shipping Calculator
complete_examples.description: Complete if/else if with multiple factors.
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Guard clause first (weight<=0). Then destination determines base tier. Within each: ternary for weight tiers. Express multiplier varies by destination. 3.5kg EU: 12.99 * 1.8 = 23.382.

Key takeaway: Real programs combine if/else, ternary, and guards for layered decisions.
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Add 'US' as a destination with higher prices. Test with different weights.

visual_diagram.title: Java Conditions -- Decision-Making Visual Guide
visual_diagram.content: JAVA CONDITIONS -- COMPLETE VISUAL GUIDE How Java makes decisions: if, else, switch, and patterns ======== [1] IF STATEMENT ======== START | v condition true? / \ YES NO | | v | {code} | | | v v CONTINUE ======== [2] IF / ELSE ======== condition? / \ YES NO | | {if-block} {else-block} | | v v CONTINUE (exactly ONE block runs) ======== [3] IF / ELSE IF / ELSE CHAIN ======== score >= 90? --YES--> Grade A | NO score >= 80? --YES--> Grade B | NO score >= 70? --YES--> Grade C | NO ELSE: Grade F First TRUE match wins. Rest SKIPPED. Order matters! ======== [4] SWITCH STATEMENT ======== switch(value) | case 1 -> action + break; case 2 -> action + break; case 3 -> action + break; default -> fallback action [!] WITHOUT BREAK = FALL-THROUGH! case 2 matches -> runs case 2, then case 3, then default! ======== [5] ENHANCED SWITCH (Java 14+) ======== String r = switch(val) { case 1 -> "One"; No break needed! case 2, 3 -> "Two/Three"; Comma groups values default -> "Other"; }; ======== [6] GUARD CLAUSES ======== INSTEAD OF: USE: if (valid) { if (!valid) return; if (ok) { if (!ok) return; if (ready) { // main logic here doStuff(); doStuff(); } } } ======== [7] WHEN TO USE WHAT ======== if/else 2 options, ranges, complex conditions else if 3+ options with ranges or complex logic switch Many exact values (int, char, String) ternary Simple 2-way value assignment guards Input validation, early exit ======== [8] COMMON BUGS ======== BUG: if (x = 5) WHY: Uses = (assign) not == (compare) FIX: if (x == 5) BUG: if (x > 5); WHY: Semicolon = empty if body FIX: if (x > 5) { BUG: Missing break in switch WHY: Fall-through to next case FIX: Add break; after each case BUG: String == instead of .equals() WHY: Compares references not content FIX: str.equals("value") BUG: Wrong else-if order WHY: >=50 before >=90 catches 95 FIX: Check most restrictive first BUG: if (active == true) WHY: Redundant comparison FIX: if (active) [REMEMBER] * if/else = exactly ONE block runs * else if chain = first TRUE match wins, rest skipped * switch needs break (or use arrow ->) * Guard clauses keep code flat * Name your booleans for readability * When in doubt, use if/else over ternary * Always use braces {} even for one-liners

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 1: Simple If -- Hot Day
practice_exercises.description: Print a message only when temperature exceeds 30. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The simplest decision! If something is true, do something.

hints.message: if (temp > 30) { System.out.println("Hot day!"); }
The condition temp > 30 is TRUE when temp=35, so the block runs.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 2: If -- Negative Number
practice_exercises.description: Check if a number is negative and print a warning. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Quick check: is the number below zero?

hints.message: if (n < 0) { ... } -- the condition is true when n is negative.
n=-5, -5<0 is TRUE, so the message prints.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 3: If/Else -- Pass or Fail
practice_exercises.description: Print PASS if score >= 50, otherwise FAIL. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Two outcomes, one choice. The classic if/else!

hints.message: if (score >= 50) { ... } else { ... }
45 >= 50 is FALSE, so else block runs: FAIL.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 4: If/Else -- Even or Odd
practice_exercises.description: Determine if a number is even or odd. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Use the modulus operator % with an if/else!

hints.message: n % 2 == 0 means even (no remainder). n=7: 7%2=1, not 0.
7%2 = 1 (not 0), so else runs: ODD.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 5: If/Else -- Absolute Value
practice_exercises.description: Print the absolute value without Math.abs. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: If negative, flip the sign. Otherwise keep it.

hints.message: if (n < 0) result = -n; else result = n;

int abs = (n < 0) ? -n : n;
System.out.println("Absolute value of " + n + " is " + abs);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 6: If/Else -- Voting Age
practice_exercises.description: Check if someone can vote (age >= 18). Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A real-world if/else: age checking!

hints.message: if (age >= 18) ... else { int wait = 18 - age; ... }
16>=18 is FALSE. 18-16=2 years to wait.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 7: If/Else -- Max of Two
practice_exercises.description: Find the larger of two numbers. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Compare two values and pick the winner!

hints.message: if (a > b) max = a; else max = b;
15 > 23 is FALSE, so else: max = 23.

int max = (a > b) ? a : b;
System.out.println("Max is " + max);

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: If -- Multiple Independent Checks
practice_exercises.description: Three separate if checks on one number. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Separate ifs are INDEPENDENT -- all three are checked!
practice_exercises.expected_output:
12 is positive
12 is even
12 is greater than 10

hints.message: Three separate if statements (not else if). All three can be true!
12>0: true. 12%2==0: true. 12>10: true. All three print.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Fix the Braces Bug
practice_exercises.description: Find why the code always prints both messages. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: A classic bug! Missing braces cause unexpected behaviour.
practice_exercises.expected_output: This should be inside the if

hints.message: Without braces, only the FIRST line is inside the if. The second line ALWAYS runs.
Add braces: if (x > 100) { ... both lines ... }

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 10: Fix the = vs == Bug
practice_exercises.description: Find the assignment-instead-of-comparison bug. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: The #1 condition bug in programming!

hints.message: found = true ASSIGNS. It should check: if (found == true) or just if (found).
The bug: found is set to true right before the check. Remove the assignment.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 11: Grade Calculator

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 19: Fix the Order Bug

hints.message: 95>=50 is TRUE, so it stops at D! Check HIGHEST first.
Reverse order: >=90 first, then >=80, >=70, >=50.
if(score>=90)grade="A";else if(score>=80)grade="B";else if(score>=70)grade="C";else if(score>=50)grade="D";else grade="F";

Without break, execution falls through ALL remaining cases.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Enhanced Switch -- Seasons

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 29: Switch vs If-Else

practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sometimes switch is cleaner than if-else. Convert this!

hints.message: switch(size) { case "S": ... } -- exact value matching is switch's strength.
If-else checks ranges. Switch matches exact values. This has exact values -> switch.

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 30: Switch Fall-Through on Purpose
practice_exercises.description: Use intentional fall-through for grouped cases. Read the starter code carefully, then complete the TODO sections.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Sometimes fall-through is a FEATURE, not a bug!

hints.message: case 1: case 2: case 3: case 4: case 5: println("Weekday"); break;
No break between 1-5 means they all fall to the same code. INTENTIONAL here!

a<b? If true, a is smaller. If false, b is smaller.
